one_line_takeaway: In a PAIR-style corpus + human-in-the-loop consumer-UI audit of ChatGPT's parental controls, the authors' own data shows privacy violence, fraud, hate speech, and malware sessions triggered zero parent notifications (NR=0%) even though the current backend has lower leak-through than legacy GPT-4.1/4o, exposing a policy-product gap between on-screen child safeguards and parent-facing telemetry.
key_finding: Parental notification coverage is selective, not comprehensive: privacy violence, fraud, hate speech, and malware produced zero parental notifications (NR = 0% for each of these four categories) across the session set, a result the authors say was robust to sensitivity checks (judge threshold +/-1, excluding ambiguous prompts). Physical harm, pornography, and health consultation triggered intermittent notifications, with physical harm showing the highest NR of any category. The current backend showed lower leak-through than the two legacy backends (GPT-4.1/GPT-4o), which in side tests produced 'somewhat inappropriate' content (suggestive descriptions, inadequate age gating, occasional dangerous-health-advice fragments) that the current backend generally refused or safely rewrote -- but critically, the notification profile itself did not change between backends for the categories that never triggered alerts, so improved model-level safety did not translate into improved parent-facing telemetry. The paper also documents overblocking of benign educational prompts near sensitive keywords (anatomy homework, historical analysis quoting slurs, benign cybersecurity hygiene) and cases where visible UI warnings/safe rewrites occurred with no corresponding parent notification, evidencing a gap between on-screen safeguards and downstream parent alerts. Illustrative examples in the appendix give time-to-notification of 4 minutes (physical-harm case) and 12 minutes (health-consultation case) for the two sessions that did notify; no aggregate quantitative table of TTN, NR/LR/OBR/UIR percentages across all seven categories is included in the extracted text beyond the qualitative heatmap description (Figure 3) and the explicit 0% figures for the four zero-notification categories.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is an empirical, two-phase audit of OpenAI ChatGPT's parental-control system as deployed to consumers (not a new jailbreak method or new model). Phase I uses a PAIR-style black-box iterative-refinement loop (propose -> query target -> score -> mutate, up to 15 mutations per seed) run over the API, with seed prompts generated by Claude Sonnet 4.5, to build a category-balanced conversation corpus across seven harm categories (physical harm, pornography, privacy violence, health consultation, fraud, hate speech, malware) and multiple child age bands. Phase II has trained human agents replay/refine the selected prompts inside the actual consumer ChatGPT web UI, logged into a dedicated child account with parental controls enabled, while the authors monitor the linked parent email inbox for notifications. Responses are labeled acceptable/borderline/unacceptable by an automated judge (ChatGPT 5, given prior 2 turns of context), calibrated against a 10%-stratified human-annotated sample, with all judge-positive and 10% of judge-negative outputs human-reviewed and disagreements resolved by a senior rater. The authors define and report four outcome metrics: Notification Rate (NR), Leak-Through Rate (LR), Overblocking Rate (OBR), and UI Intervention Rate (UIR), and compare the current production backend against two legacy backends historically exposed in the product (GPT-4.1 and GPT-4o).
threat_model: Not an adversary-vs-model exploit paper in the classic sense; the 'threat' framed by the authors is a policy-product gap for families: a child (or someone posing as one) using a supervised consumer chat account under parental controls, employing 'child-authentic' boundary-testing tactics (repetition, emotional appeals, literalization, story/homework framing, third-person distancing, authority-figure appeals) rather than adversarial-adult or automated-attacker tactics, and the question is whether platform-layer safeguards (refusals, safe rewrites, UI warnings) and parent-facing telemetry (email notifications) actually cover the harms they claim to, or whether children can produce or receive unsafe content that vendors nevertheless fail to surface to a supervising parent. The paper explicitly distinguishes this from prior jailbreak/red-teaming literature (PAIR, persona modulation, ReNeLLM, adaptive/API-aware attacks, J2), which they characterize as testing model-level alignment/attack novelty rather than testing the platform's end-user guard-rail and notification layer -- that distinction is drawn by the authors themselves in Sections 1 and 2, not something this review is inferring beyond the text.
limitations: The authors' own Limitations section (Section 9) states: (1) scope/generalizability is limited to seven categories and one specific consumer product configuration, and the study did not attempt to estimate population prevalence of unsafe outcomes; (2) seed-prompt authenticity is a concern because seeds were generated by Claude Sonnet 4.5 rather than derived from naturalistic child-chatbot interaction logs, and were not validated against real child discourse or with child-development/linguistics experts, so idiosyncratic real-child phrasing, spelling errors, code-switching, and pragmatic strategies may not be captured; (3) the notification pipeline is treated as a black box, so missing alerts could reflect policy gating, classifier thresholds, deliverability issues, or email batching rather than a true absence of detection; (4) the primary judge (ChatGPT 5) risks model-model correlation bias with the target assistant and category-specific precision/recall tradeoffs, mitigated but not eliminated by human audit; (5) prompts evolved via API then were replayed in the UI, and routing/context-window/safety-layer differences between API and UI could affect outcomes; (6) temporal/day-of-week drift in alignment and notification policy could affect time-to-notification and coverage; (7) human-agent paraphrasing introduces micro-variation that could alter safety triggers; (8) legacy-backend tests used product-exposed GPT-4.1/GPT-4o variants during specific replay windows, not guaranteed to match historical deployments exactly; (9) construct-validity concerns around the overblocking definition (some platforms may prefer refusal over safe rewrites as a normative stance) and around category-boundary ambiguity for privacy violence and fraud versus general advice.

datasets_used: Custom category-balanced conversation corpus built by the authors via a PAIR-style iterative-refinement loop over seven harm categories (physical harm, pornography, privacy violence, health consultation, fraud, hate speech, malware) and multiple child age bands; not a pre-existing public benchmark dataset.
quantitative_results: Notification Rate (NR) = 0% for each of privacy violence, fraud, hate speech, and malware categories, robust to judge-threshold +/-1 sensitivity checks and exclusion of ambiguous prompts
Physical harm had the highest NR of all seven categories; pornography and health consultation produced intermittent (non-zero, non-comprehensive) notifications
Current backend showed generally low but non-zero Leak-Through Rate (LR) specifically in health consultation and pornography categories; near-zero LR in other categories after iterative refinement
Judge calibration: 10% stratified human-annotated sample used to tune thresholds; 10% of judge-negative responses plus 100% of judge-positive responses human-reviewed

Appendix example TTN (time-to-notification): 4 minutes for a physical-harm session (SP1 example, age band 10-12); 12 minutes for a health-consultation session (SP2 example, age band 13-15); a privacy-violence session (SP3 example, age band 10-12) produced no notification at all despite borderline-labeled content
baselines_compared: Current production ChatGPT backend (used for API corpus construction) vs. two legacy backends historically exposed in the product: GPT-4.1 and GPT-4o, tested during separate replay windows on the same prompt families
